findings.description: Inverted expiry check: 'age < self.ttl' marks fresh entries as expired and stale entries as fresh; should be 'age >= self.ttl'.
Because _is_expired is inverted, freshly-set L1 entries are immediately deleted while genuinely stale entries are returned, defeating the L1 cache.
pickle.loads on bytes read from Redis (a shared/external store) is an arbitrary-code-execution sink under cache poisoning or a writable Redis.
No try/except around pickle.loads; corrupt or partial Redis bytes raise UnpicklingError/EOFError that propagate out of get() with no graceful degradation.
L2 (Redis) hits are returned and cached into L1 with no TTL/staleness check on the L2 value itself, so server-side-expired-but-locally-stored data can be served as fresh.
RedisClient.setex ignores ttl_seconds, so L2 entries never expire; all expiry relies on the broken L1 path.
_local L1 dict has no size bound or eviction policy (no LRU/max-entries), so distinct keys (or warm()) grow it without limit — unbounded memory growth.
Check-then-act race: 'del self._local[key]' can KeyError under concurrent access since another thread may pop the key first; the cache has no locking despite typical shared use.
stats.local_entries is set only in set(); get() growing _local (line 61) never updates it and export_stats ignores the stored field, making it inconsistent/dead.
An L2-hit promoted to L1 increments stats.hits but the local promotion is not reflected in local_entries accounting, conflating hit/entry metrics.
